---
title: "3.2 Impact of Program Design"
subtitle: "Class Creation"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit3-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Impact of Program Design

### Programs affect people, systems, and decisions

This topic keeps the textbook focus on how software work reaches beyond code quality alone.

::: {.tight-list}
- programs operate in real social settings
- developers should consider <span class="term">harm</span>, <span class="term">privacy</span>, and reliability
- technical choices can produce both intended and unintended consequences
:::

## System Reliability

### Reliable software performs as expected under stated conditions

<span class="term">System reliability</span> means a program can perform its tasks without failure under the conditions it was designed for.

::: {.tight-list}
- reliability does not happen by accident
- programmers improve reliability through <span class="mark">testing with a variety of conditions</span>
- edge cases matter because failures often appear outside the normal path
:::

## Beneficial and Harmful Impacts

### The same program can solve one problem and create another

The textbook keeps two ideas together:

::: {.tight-list}
- programs can improve life, work, communication, safety, and access
- programs can also cause unintended harmful effects beyond their intended use
- impacts reach society, the economy, and culture
:::

Students should evaluate software by looking at both benefits and risks.

## Legal Use and Open Source

### Reusing code still carries responsibility

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">Open source</span> code is published for reuse under its license terms
- code that is <span class="mark">not</span> open source may require permission or purchase
- intellectual property and legal constraints are part of program design work
:::

Using existing code is normal, but using it correctly is part of professional practice.

## AI and Ethical Decisions

### Technical systems may force hard choices

The textbook uses self-driving cars as a concrete example.

::: {.tight-list}
- AI and machine learning can improve safety and efficiency
- those same systems may face ethical tradeoffs
- questions about responsibility do not disappear when decisions are automated
:::

Students should be ready to discuss who is accountable: the software, the organization, or the programmers who built it.

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- evaluate software applications for beneficial and harmful effects
- discuss how testing relates to <span class="term">system reliability</span>
- distinguish open-source reuse from code that needs permission
- examine ethical issues in AI within a specific domain
- <span class="term">3.2.1 Groupwork: Impacts of CS</span>
:::

The linked videos in the textbook work as optional openers, not required core deck content.

## Classroom Check

### A strong answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- define <span class="term">system reliability</span> accurately
- explain why testing under varied conditions matters
- describe both beneficial and harmful impacts of a program
- identify the difference between open-source reuse and restricted code use
- discuss one ethical issue raised by AI in a concrete application area
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
